summary: "Documentation should specify the API/Specification that formats are implemented against"
description: """With some of the formats that Nexus supports, there are many clients and many ways that they can interact with the registry.    One example would be Helm, wherein the v2 commands and v3 commands are different and there is also a plugin ecosystem that users may commonly use.    Whilst it is not feasible to specify all clients and systems for a format that may be supported, there needs to be a way to know what *should* work.    To this end, the documentation should ideally specify the API and/or specification(s) for the format that Nexus has implemented, such that users can more easily determine if certain functionality should work, or is simple unsupported/unimplemented.    This would be beneficial for all formats where a formal API or specification exists."""

summary: "Better explain that you can't publish to proxies/groups for formats"
description: """Scenario:   # A user sets up required tooling to use a proxy or group (more than likely a group)   # User attempts to publish/deploy/etc... to URL   ## Nexus Repo rightfully denies this   # User spends next few minutes or hours Googling to try and figure out what they've messed up with their config, rather than realizing this is by design    Some thing I stumble upon in the community role is that our users try and publish to proxies and groups more often than they should. I think that we could be a bit more explicit about this being impossible, at least until we did something for groups where you can publish and it picks the first hosted repo (I remember a ticket like this).    Example of confusion: [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37856845/405-error-on-maven-build-to-nexus-3/40618154#40618154]    Acceptance Criteria:   * Maybe we run the documentation changes by the previously confused users and see if it is more clear to them that you cannot publish to a proxy/group"""

summary: "Proxy repositories should include their remote URL in the repository view"
description: """As a developer, I would like to be able to view the remote URL of proxy repositories, so that I can know exactly what they are for.         *Details*    Presently, it is not possible for non-administrators to view the remote url of proxy repositories. This provides a poor user experience, and—especially with a large number of repositories—necessitates documenting what each one does in a separate place.    Proxy repositories should show the remote URL on their page so that users can make informed decisions about what they're using. Additionally, proxy repositories should be searchable by their remote URL so that users can easily find suitable internal repositories.         _Note_: It's possible to grant users access to this information with the {{nx-repository-admin-*-*-read}} privilege, however, this is far from an ideal solution."""

summary: "Move components to cheaper storage"
description: """Customers have expressed the need to be able to move components to cheaper storage.  At the moment, this can only be done by exporting and manually deleting them. There are lots of ways this could be improved:   * Manually triggered, e.g. staging move into a repo attached to cheap storage   * A policy that grabs components automatically, e.g. after they're a certain age   * Configured at the repo level (e.g. some repos are attached to cheap storage)   * Configured at the blob store level (e.g. a blob store group with a policy that auto-migrates really old content into a cheaper blob store within the group)   * Components continue to be available, vs. components that disappear from the repo and are inaccessible and need to be brought back in, either by external intervention or automatically by NXRM (possibly with some delay)."""
comments: "I see this is still unassigned, has this gotten much traction to get on the projects list?" [SEP] "[~<USER> This ticket describes a broad problem rather than a specific solution, but we have been chipping away at some of the use cases - for example, the 'staging move' operation works between members of a repo group, and we'll soon be releasing a task that lets you move a repository between blob stores, so you can unpick a 'single blob store' strategy if you need to.    What is your particular need?" [SEP] "I believe once we have the Change Repo Blob Store NEXUS-12016 functionality, it will cover the use case of this improvement request."

summary: "cleanup by age criteria should work against assets even if they are not part of components"
description: """Maven 2 repos allow a """"Permissive"""" layout policy. Maven 2 repos can also be created with a """"Mixed"""" version policy ( -NEXUS-17723- ).    Cleanup by using age criteria ( last downloaded, deployed, uploaded) select """"components"""" to operate on and apply age criteria. Some assets that are deployed under a permissive layout policy are not pure """"components"""", only """"assets"""", therefore may never get cleaned up under normal cleanup tasks that Nexus provides. The new cleanup policies we have rely on the Elastic Search index which operates on components, not database queries against assets.    The only workaround for cleaning such leftover assets is to use a groovy script to select these items from the database.  h4. Expected    Provide a built in mechanism to cleanup assets purely by age, irregardless of whether they are part of a component.     """

summary: "optimize S3 blobstores for intelligent tiering"
description: """AWS S3 supports various object storage classes.     By default the S3 Blobstore implementation uses multi-part HTTP uploads of S3 objects with the default Storage Class of """"STANDARD"""" for all objects.    AWS S3 supports a feature called intelligent tiering of objects which dynamically tiers objects. To fully make use of this, one can set the storage class explicitly for objects when they are uploaded to INTELLIGENT_TIERING. Currently repository manager does not offer any configurability or optimization for using intelligent tiering storage classes.    From https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/storage-class-intro.html#sc-dynamic-data-access :    {quote}  Storage Class That Automatically Optimizes Frequently and Infrequently Accessed Objects  The INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class is designed to optimize storage costs by automatically moving data to the most cost-effective storage access tier, without performance impact or operational overhead. INTELLIGENT_TIERING delivers automatic cost savings by moving data on a granular object level between two access tiers, a frequent access tier and a lower-cost infrequent access tier, when access patterns change. The INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class is ideal if you want to optimize storage costs automatically for long-lived data when access patterns are unknown or unpredictable.    The INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class stores objects in two access tiers: one tier that is optimized for frequent access and another lower-cost tier that is optimized for infrequently accessed data. For a small monthly monitoring and automation fee per object, Amazon S3 monitors access patterns of the objects in the INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class and moves objects that have not been accessed for 30 consecutive days to the infrequent access tier. There are no retrieval fees when using the INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class. If an object in the infrequent access tier is accessed, it is automatically moved back to the frequent access tier. No additional tiering fees apply when objects are moved between access tiers within the INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class.    Note    The INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class is suitable for objects larger than 128 KB that you plan to store for at least 30 days. If the size of an object is less than 128 KB, it is not eligible for auto-tiering. Smaller objects can be stored, but they are always charged at the frequent access tier rates in the INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class. If you delete an object before the 30-day minimum, you are charged for 30 days. For pricing information, see Amazon S3 Pricing.    {quote}      h4. References    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/storage-class-intro.html#sc-howtoset  https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2018/11/s3-intelligent-tiering/  https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/new-automatic-cost-optimization-for-amazon-s3-via-intelligent-tiering/  https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html  https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingMetadata.html#object-metadata  https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTObjectPUT.html    h4. Expected    Allow optimization of S3 blobstore objects for storing intelligent tiering type storage classes.    What this actually means is yet to be defined - it could be as simple as allowing configurability of storage classes per blobstore, or something a little more dynamic.    h4. Using S3 Lifecycle Rules Instead    S3 Lifecycle Rules may be a workaround to implementing something in product. See    See these links for using a lifecycle policy to handle transitioning objects to various tiers of storage classes:    https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/new-automatic-cost-optimization-for-amazon-s3-via-intelligent-tiering/  https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html  """

summary: "REST API rate limiting"
description: """As the implemented REST endpoints expand, production NXRM instances become more susceptible to DoS attacks. Some sort of rate limiting could protect an NXRM instance from unintentional or intentional DoS attacks usually caused by scripts written against NXRM endpoints.    h4. Expected    Implement a request rate limiting feature for REST API endpoints.    h4. Workaround    Reverse proxy servers in front of NXRM may have a rate limiting feature that could be used instead:    https://www.nginx.com/blog/rate-limiting-nginx/  """
comments: "Implementing NEXUS-20546 would help further protect API endpoints by limiting their access to specific authenticated users - however would not address rogue scripts using the credentials of privileged users."

summary: "Allow for removal of repository access from Anonymous user/role"
description: """Some large enterprises have certain repositories in their instances that they wish to hide from the rest of the organization (contents are sensitive). Current approach is to create a 'custom' Anonymous role and assign it to the Anonymous user. This can be tedious as you have to add each privilige to this role and have to do this every time a new repository is created.    Instead, it would be a lot easier if the default Anonymous role could be specified which repositories it should not have any access to."""
comments: "I think this ticket could be generalized to just allow negative access.  This could be valuable outside the anonymous usage as well (though good to have the use case).    I also wonder if this could be worked-around somehow with content selectors either now or maybe in future endeavor."

summary: "add hard quota for blobstores"
description: """I would like to pick up following request in the mailling list, because we have a similiar problem:  [https://groups.google.com/a/glists.sonatype.com/d/topic/nexus-users/Yi-VF7skaLM/discussion]    We have several different projects on our plattform that are using our Nexus instance as artifact repository for the development. It is not intended to be used for archiving purposes. We periodically have the issue that our backup space for release repositories runs out and we have to ask the projects which artifact versions are obsolete (e.g. they never were released to the customer or they are out of service) and can be removed. We also order new (quite expensive) expansion of our storage, if it's necessary.    With the blobstores that were introduced with Nexus 3 we get the possibility to better organize the used storage of each project. However we still have the problem to monitor the disk space in order to prevent system failures due to missing disk space.   With the feature to restrict the maximum size of blobstores, we would gain the possibility to define capacity quotas for each project and ensure the stability for other projects when one project violates them."""
comments: "[~<USER> FYI, we have a similar issue in our internal backlog (currently private) so I made this issue public so others could see and vote on it." [SEP] "Hi,    If you add <USER>limit for repository is helpful, soft limit is simply warning to user and simply adding more logs to log file. All user feel like enable hardlimit for each repository then it will be good. Please check on this. Thank you"

summary: "reduce JVM heap memory consumed by group repository 404 not found responses"
description: """AbstractGroupRepository collects any throwables thrown from its member repositories when requesting content and stores them in a map (memberThrowables) which it then propagates upstream inside a GroupItemNotFoundException. The stored throwables are only used when handling 'describe' requests.    For complex setups with lots of nested groups and many concurrent requests these throwables can end up taking a significant amount of heap, only never to be used because the requests are almost exclusively non-describe requests.    By skipping collection of these throwables for non-describe requests we should reduce pressure on the heap for all users (those with complex setups should see the most benefit)."""

summary: "add base staging suite like features for repository manager 3.x"
description: """This initial collection of work makes available a base set of staging suite features in the Nexus Repository Manager PRO product line. The staging functionality is exposed by way of a set of REST APIs and includes support for tagging components and moving components between repositories.     This set of APIs and capabilities provides a set of flexible building blocks that can be combined and orchestrated as needed to accomodate your organization's software build and release pipeline and can be easily integrated into CI/CD systems to support established workflows.    Additionally, new security permissions and a schedulable task have also been provided to support staging activities.    """

summary: "LDAP group membership matching should be case insensitive by default"
description: """LDAP group membership matching is case-sensitive inside Nexus Repository Manager. In rare cases some LDAP servers systems may enforce group matching case-sensitively, but by default DN and attribute matching is case-insensitive:    [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/29897684/is-ldap-dn-case-insensitive]    This issue is to acknowledge the application default matching algorithm and provide a vehicle to gather user feedback if change the default is warranted/desired by users.    Changing the default matching could conceivably result in a security escalation in rare circumstances, so such a change could require a major version bump of product.    The IQ Server also appears to match case sensitively on group membership as well, so both server product defaults are consistent to my knowledge.     """

summary: "feature to always prevent mapping external LDAP groups into local or externally defined user accounts"
description: """I would like external ldap mapping roles not assignable to users, as the mapping should be done by ldap groups. Unfortunately Nexus 3 does not have this restriction.    In Nexus 3, I can assign an externally mapped role to a ldap user,  overruling whatever I specified in the LDAP. This can make it very confusing.    h4. Summary    Provide a feature to always prevent mapping external LDAP groups into local or externally defined user accounts."""
comments: "Historically this mapping of external LDAP groups into externally mapped LDAP users has been possible in all versions of Nexus.    Since enforcing restrictions on both Nexus administration and LDAP administration is possible by standard security best practice, and changing the current behaviour would introduce regressions to existing behaviour, we have no plans to change this in product.    This was the first and only request for such a feature. "

summary: "Staging Rule - Uniqueness Check That Allows Identical Duplicates"
description: """I would like to ask if it was possible to add another staging rule, that would work very similarly to the standard uniqueness check (that prevents closing/promoting staging profile if the artifact is already in the system), however it would not fail if the artifact was already present in the system but was binary equal.     This would enable possibility of deployment of multiple projects, that share dependency on identical common artifacts (it is not always possible to make separate repository with the shared content).  """

According to Maven, a component is 'equal' if the coordinates are the same.    Please elaborate - are you saying you want a rule that ignores coordinates of staged artifacts and released artifacts and instead looks for components that match the checksum of staged components?" We sometimes need to work with several product versions in staging suite, that contain dependency on a common artifact. We need the  artifact to appear in the target repository group. However with the current uniqueness check we can only enable the first promotion of the common artifact while all other cases would fail - the artifact is already in the system.    If we remove the common artifact from the second, third etc. staging repository, they can

summary: "/service/metrics/ping and healthcheck shouldn't require authentication"
description: """Nexus offers 4 metrics endpoints:   * \{host:port}/service/metrics/healthcheck   * \{host:port}/service/metrics/data   * \{host:port}/service/metrics/ping   * \{host:port}/service/metrics/threads    While it is arguable that data & threads should need protection, ping & healtcheck surely don't.  At the moment, user authentcation is required to query these endpoints, it would be great if this was not needed, it would simplify configuration in monitoring & load-balancing system."""
comments: "See here:         [https://support.sonatype.com/hc/en-us/articles/226254487-Nexus-3-Server-Metrics-and-Health-Information]"

summary: "NuGet local repositories should support API Search() by targetFramework"
description: """Work on NEXUS-6158 has been focused on solving the immediate issue of broken builds caused by lack of support for the targetFramework attribute. This issue represents the remainder of the work no longer encompassed by that issue, including:    1. Capture of targetFramework attributes for hosted repos in a way that they can be easily used in queries from the Search() API endpoint. This should likely entail consideration of how to represent compatible targetFramework versions per the NuGet specification as part of the data model.    2. Implementation of the Search() api method's targetFramework query parameter in a way consistent with the NuGet specification, including the aforementioned issues of determining compatible targetFrameworks.    3. As we're not capturing this information yet in searchable form, we may need to implement a means of migrating data from the dependencies attribute (now containing the targetFramework data in the format expected by NuGet) to the new targetFrameworks attribute we'll need to use for searching. If we don't do something, users that start utilizing NuGet hosted following NEXUS-6158 will end up with gaps in returned results (or they'll have to redeploy their artifacts to pick up these changes, which may or may not appear unseemly).  """

summary: "Content selectors by tag"
description: """As a user of content selectors, I'd like to be able to include tag == <tag> in my CSEL queries.    This ticket is to gauge interest and understand use cases.  Please vote if this interests your company."""
comments: "Any use case information is welcome.  I envisioned: 1) less compexity 2) multiple repos in a single assignment 3) multiple formats in a single assignment.  These are all varients on the same theme, I think." [SEP] "My thoughts are that this would provide a useful method for promoting assets through whatever testing/staging/production environments exist solely by updating a tag rather than moving an asset around multiple repositories."

summary: "Apply Codahale metrics to Facets"
description: """There's a limitation in codahale's guice integration that restricts the use of @Timed (and related) annotations to plain components. It won't instrument assisted-inject factories.    As a result, we can't use Codahale annotations for facet methods, which is big limitation given NX3's repository architecture."""
comments: "[~<USER> wondered if the actual issue was that facets aren't singletons; they're not assisted-inject, only the repo is."

summary: "Make it possible to enforce that only staging operations can promote release artifacts"
description: """A staging move operation requires the following privileges:     * """"nx-repository-view-<repo-format>-<repoId>-delete"""" privilege in the source repository   * """"nx-repository-view-<repo-format>-<repoId>-add"""" privilege in the destination repository    So a user that can perform the staging operation implicitly has to have write access to the target repository.  This is not desirable.  It should be possible to enforce that users must use staging to move artifacts into the target repository.  Without this, it is possible for users making deployments to bypass the staging workflows set up by companies.    Note that NEXUS-18917 is related to this request.  """
comments: "This functionality is needed by our company to ensure that proper promotions take place." [SEP] "Would definitely like this feature. Without this, a ""prod"" repo is just like any other repo in Nexus whether it's dev, QA, etc. A user can bypass workflows and upload directly to the ""prod"" repo. Not great for audit and compliance as well." [SEP] "Our company needs functionality like this and https://issues.sonatype.org/browse/NEXUS-18917 as well. Otherwise, as @Phillip said, it doesn't work for audit and compliance." [SEP] "Just a note to say that this is a sensible improvement; it's not something we have scheduled imminently, but we are watching demand for it accumulate with interest."

summary: "eliminate soft-deleted blobs and reduce transaction retries for identical proxy repository asset requests"
description: """*Background*    In highly concurrent situations, multiple clients may request the same content from an NXRM proxy repository. In these cases, the parallel requests significant churn on the Asset record, with unnecessarily large numbers of blobs created as a result of transaction retries fighting over which blob to attach to it. This could occur in multiple HA nodes using the same blobstore, or on a single node (multiple threads). Because the soft-deleted blobs from the unsuccessful tries linger until the blob store is compacted, this can also waste storage space.    *Acceptance*    Detect when a transaction is attempting to attach a blob to an Asset when an identical blob already exists on that Asset. In those cases, ignore the attach request, and retain the existing blob.     """

summary: "Ext JS browse UI tree view"
description: """h2. What problem are we solving?    PE-49 reintroduces the concept of asset path hierarchy via a tree browsing service (and accompanying HTML views). However, our Ext JS browse UI still has a few remaining problems:    * It isn’t taking advantage of that new service.  * It has separate features for component browsing and asset browsing. This is overly complicated.  * There is significant pogo sticking in the current drilldown-based design. This makes browsing a repo more difficult than it should be.    h2. How will we measure success?    There are currently 24 votes across the two sister issues (NEXUS-12518 and NEXUS-12531). We will validate the changes with everyone who voted. If successful, 90%+ of the people we talk to will indicate that our solution addresses their needs.    h2. What do we need to do?    * NXRM users can open up a tree-like view (e.g. as a peer nav option to Browse)  * Identify if there are scalability issues (e.g. when a single node has 50k children, as could happen in a Git LFS or docker repository).  ** Scalability issues might be dealt with in other stories, and potentially quite simply - perhaps by truncation. (Who's going to tree through 50k nodes?)  * If you expand a node with multiple hops (containing only a single directory each), concatenate the names into a single node (so org > *apache* > *activemq* would become org > *apache/activemq*, assuming org has many children).  * We show assets in the tree as leaf nodes  ** If you select an asset, the asset detail view opens  ** If you select a component, the component detail view opens  *** Because we’re showing assets in the tree, we should remove the asset list from the component detail view  * The asset and component detail views use side-panels, not the current drilldown pattern    !tree_and_detail_panel.png|width=600!"""

summary: "repository size quotas"
description: """We have teams going ROUGUE with building and has shut nexus down.    If a team could be limited to 10 gb for a repository and be blocked with a message to the curl feedback; that would be ideal"""
comments: "[~ej] Thanks for this improvement suggestion. Incidentally, have you seen our survey about cleanup?    https://blog.sonatype.com/nexus-repository-manager-retention-policies-and-cleanup-survey" [SEP] "We have the same need. In our scenario each team get a raw repo where they can store large files that they need for their builds etc. Some teams are very active in storing but less good at removing, so it would be good to set a quota to force them to remove things they don't need any more.  A workaround, that we haven't explored, would be to create a separate blob store for each raw repo as soft quotas can be set on blob stores. But we'd rather just have one blob store (less config and management) and set the quota on repo level."

summary: "Go Hosted repository"
description: """As a user of Nexus Repository Manager, I would like to store my go components (in addition to Proxy and Group).    Public ticket to gauge interest and potential Q&A/public requirements."""

In our corporate Go development world we have been managing our go dependencies manually but this is no longer feasible nor maintainable due to the number of projects we now have on the Go (couldn't resist).    I've been looking into the Athens server as a potential GOPROXY and it is promising; I have a local environment with it setup and it works very well; however, I don't think Athens can be the solution within our corporate infrastructure because it means another piece of server software that our IT department has to integrate and maintain.    We are already using Nexus (very successfully!) to host and serve most of our build artifacts (Docker, Node, Java, Python, etc...) and it would be a huge advantage for us to be able to leverage Nexus for our Go modules as well.    Thanks for the consideration." [SEP] "Thanks for the message [~<USER> support for proxy Go remotes using GOPROXY will be released in 3.17 (https://issues.sonatype.org/browse/NEXUS-13785). We're expectin

summary: "SPIKE - Migration approach for maven2/yum hybrid builds"
description: """NXRM2 Yum users can deploy their Maven and Yum artifacts into a single repository because the Yum implementation in NXRM2 is built on Maven. In NXRM3 we have built Yum as a first-class citizen and therefore don't have all of the Maven logic. Any users migrating who are using maven to make and deploy RPMs will need a recommendation.    *Acceptance criteria*   * Produce a recommendation of the best migration approach   * e.g.   ** Tough luck, redo your build - as you would if you were doing maven2 jars built into a docker build   ** Come up with a fancy maven trick that's minimally invasive for customers   *** e.g. forking the deploy plugin that knows how to split   ** Implement hybrid repos in nxrm 3   *** for real   *** NXRM has a diverter route (as a capability?) for maven repos that can move yum assets somewhere else   *** Use a reverse proxy to fake a hybrid repo (KB article), but diverting content behind the scenes"""

summary: "Nexus staging should support having repos pre-pended or post-pended"
description: """Nexus 2's staging system always appends (post-pend) a new staging repository in the staging group. This means that new staged artifacts may be occluded by older artifacts in previous staging repositories.    It would be good if this could be configured to be a pre-pend order or not. It be great if this was available for Nexus 2 (since it already has staging) but definitely for Nexus 3. As it is, I have to run a cron job which forcibly fixes the order but this means there's a race condition present on the repository ordering."""

summary: "configurable header validation"
description: """Nexus 3.2.0 added header validation to prevent certain attack vectors, for example making sure the host header contains a value that matches IP addresses, host names and port values, and patterns according to RFC-1123.    In a customer security conscious environment, it is desirable to restrict NXRM further, to accept an even more stringent set of values.    h4. Expected    A Nexus administrator should be able to configure patterns, ideally regex based, that will be used to match against incoming HTTP header values. If a header exists, and the value of the header does not match all of the whitelisted patterns, then the request will be rejected with an error before performing any other processing.    Any default patterns that NXRM already validates against need not be circumvented, as default patterns Nexus uses are for the protection of the server in general. The improvement is about making validation even more restrictive - for example only allowing a few specific host names in the host header.    Header values which aggregate from multiple sources ( ie. Host, X-Forwarded-Host, Jetty org.eclipse.jetty.server.ForwardedRequestCustomizer.setHostHeader() ) should also be considered."""

summary: "add anonymous read access support for docker repositories"
description: """Users would like to have anonymous read (pull) access to docker repositories in Nexus. This helps consume and share docker images more easily by not requiring a specific login. This is analogous to the benefits offered by anonymous access by other formats.    As an end user, I don't want to have to configure authentication for read only access to docker repositories.  Docker hub does not require this, and neither should Nexus Repository Manager.    According to this comment from a Docker developer the correct way to do this would be to implement token authentication, and to have Nexus hand out tokens for anonymous access:    https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/24129#issuecomment-230610547    There might also be a simpler implementation that should be considered for blanket anonymous access to a repository.  """
comments: "We need Docker repositories to be configurable for both login and anonymous access.  Only certain individuals should be able to push images while various users and systems (e.g. PasS, OpenShift, etc.) are much cleaner with appropriate anonymous access.  We achieved this functionality with the Artifactory product by creating an anonymous, read-only alias repository for a protected one." [SEP] "This currently is a blocker for the adoption of nexus for us." [SEP] "This is another issue for us as well.  Please put this on your board.  " [SEP] "This is also blocking our desired usecase. We want to transparently proxy the dockerhup registry by using a public docker repo group including dockerhub and our private docker repos.  Setting up the local docker daemons with {{--registry-mirror=https://my.server.de:6000}} should behave when doing {{docker pull namespace/myimage}} following:  * first try to find the image in hosted repos  * if not found try to fetch from dockerhub    This is not possible at the moment, sinc

summary: "Make it possible to use a repository partitioning strategy to restrict tagging"
description: """Currently the tagging implementation in Nexus Repo 3 is largely incompatible with partitioning of repositories by permissions.  The reason for this is that tagging requires just two privileges:   * nx-tags-associate   * nx-repository-view-?-?-browse    It's not that common to restrict what a developer can view, but it is quite common to restrict what they can read and write.  So for most users of tagging there is currently no way to limit which components a user is allowed to tag.  Restrictions are important when you consider that tags are often used in staging.  Tags which are on the wrong components can cause the wrong components to be moved during a staging operation.    There needs to be a way to restrict what a user can tag in a Nexus repository that is not based on browse privileges.  One possibility would be to require write privileges on a component before tagging will work.    Also note that there is currently a bug which makes it so that even for users that are willing to restrict browse privileges partitioning still won't work: NEXUS-18898          """
comments: "This functionality is needed by our company to ensure that proper tagging of promotions take place."

summary: "Move persisted firewall transaction queue from config to component database for mitigating performance issues"
description: """Both {{firewall_transaction}} and {{firewall_transaction_sequence}} ( related to Nexus Firewall feature ) are located in the _config_ database. This data is mutated frequently and really belongs in the _component_ database next to the components and assets which are being analysed. Having it in the _config_ database means that database is modified much more often than it needs to be (which has implications for backup and restore) and it's also sync'd separately to the component db changes.    Having the firewall transaction queue persisted in the config database (rather than the more natural location of the component database) leads to timing issues and inconsistencies under load. This is because changes to the config DB are replicated across the cluster by different OrientDB worker threads compared to the component DB.    For example, a request to evaluate a new asset (referenced by its encoded entity id) could be queued up on the firewall queue and replicated across the cluster via the config DB workers before that asset record is replicated across the cluster by the component DB workers. Part of this is because we set the quorum to """"majority"""" which means that a change is accepted if a majority of nodes agree with it - this then implies that the minority node could be temporarily inconsistent, but will eventually become consistent. It's during this window that a firewall request could be queued up before all the nodes have the asset record.    This improvement will move the persisted firewall transaction data to the component database, by changing the current entity adapter to use the component database and by adding an upgrade step to move over existing data. Note that it might not be necessary to move over all data."""

summary: "Limit multiple outbound upstream requests for the same proxied asset"
description: """NX3 currently allows multiple requests for the same proxied asset to go upstream, one of which will be the """"winner"""" when the storage transaction is committed. This avoids any upfront locking, improving scalability - especially in a distributed setup. The downside is the potential for duplicated work, which will end up being thrown away for any """"losing"""" threads.    This story will attempt to limit the amount duplicated proxy work, while maintaining scalability."""

summary: "align user token content protection for non-content requests"
description: """The original intent of the [NXRM user token|https://help.sonatype.com/repomanager3/system-configuration/user-authentication/security-setup-with-user-tokens] content protection feature was as a way to discourage a build tool or user of that tool from storing corporate plain credentials in a file on disk.    With the advent of repository formats where obtaining a format specific token is normal operation using realms ( npm/Docker), these format specific tokens somewhat obviate the need to use the NXRM User Token. Meaning the NXRM user token is not normally needed when fetching actual repository content ( assets / components ).     Therefore for consistency across all repository formats and format specific token realms:    -  the only case where a request should fail with 401 due to NXRM user token content protection feature is if that specific HTTP request asked for content and attempted authentication in the same request using plain text ( non-token) credentials to a /repository/* content download endpoint.  - allowing non-content operations, such as when authenticating with plain text credentials to obtain a format specific token ( ie. docker login / npm login ) for subsequent use should be allowed to succeed ( no corporate creds are stored on client disk, only format specific token), as no actual content is being requested in that case  - any existing inconsistencies to the above two statements should be aligned across all formats/repos that have token realms, despite changes introduced in NEXUS-16159 in version 3.22.0        """

summary: "Replace audit database with simple log based implementation"
description: """We've seen repeated instances over the years where the current auditing implementation has imposed significant overhead on Nexus Repo, and we've had to ask customers to disable it.  This is unfortunate, because the very customers who would want auditing the most (our largest customers) often cannot use it.    Nexus IQ Server has recently implemented auditing using a simple *log file based implementation*.  We've had zero complaints about this from customers so far about this simple implementation.    I propose that we eliminate the current audit database and UI, and replace it with a log based implementation.   By moving to a lightweight implementation we could even have it *enabled by default*, which would be tremendously helpful when trying to solve customer problems.          """
comments: "As noted in the description, audit database is no longer in use, and now we use audit log files.  Just keep in mind that users are expected to manually remove the audit database files ($work-dir/db/audit) from disk.  This was not done as an upgrade step in case there is any information in the database that the user may want to keep for some reason or another"

summary: "provide orientdb connection pool information in support zip / jmx mbean"
description: """Reports have been made where Nexus has become non-responsive. Thread dumps suggest that in some cases threads were blocked or backing up waiting for connections from the Orient DB connection pool. This is not at the point where database queries are timing out - the diagnosis point is getting connections from the pool to make the query. Such conditions can be created if a poorly written Groovy script is not cleaning up after itself after throwing an exception and not closing transactions.    In order to make diagnosing the database connection pool state easier, adding an MBean that gets dumped into the jmx.json that captures the current state of the connection pool our database queries use would be a good idea and help us prove or disprove connection pool current state and provide guidance for possible adjustment of the pool."""

summary: "Specify arbitrary Smart Proxy host for subscribers to connect on"
description: """Right now, Smart Proxy uses the machine's reported FQDN to determine which host subscribers should connect to.    We have Nexus proxy instances:  * proxy1.internal.example.com  * proxy2.internal.example.com    These  behind a load balancer:   * maven.example.com    Smart Proxy subscribers hitting maven.example.com are told to connect to proxy1.internal.example.com, which is intentionally not resolvable.    Our current solution is to tell the proxy instances that their FQDN is maven.example.com, but this feels like a workaround.    Being able to set the Smart Proxy host, like we can with the port, would address this problem."""
comments: "The smartproxy connector ""advertise uri"" is meant for this purpose.  Or at least the purpose here is to expose the host:port to use when the local host/naming might be internal.    Smartproxy however can not be load balanced between nodes.  So fronting a set of nodes with a load-balancer and using smartproxy while expecting this to function is not sane." [SEP] "Thanks Jason. Looks like I should've raised a support request instead of a NEXUS issue..." [SEP] "Hi <USER>    I see that Jason has responded to your ticket. In future, if you have any further queries, please raise a new ticket in https://support.sonatype.com.    Kind Regards,  Mahendra    "

summary: "Provide more reasonable documentation regarding pros and cons of blob storage in NXRM 3"
description: """Currently, the documentation at [https://help.sonatype.com/repomanager3/configuration/repository-management#RepositoryManagement-BlobStores] makes some generalizations that do not take into consideration things such as scheduled tasks.  As blobstores currently lack the ability to be split, migrated, or further managed after the repository has already been assigned, would be wise to take a slightly different approach to this as it will affect customer experiences overall.         See:    [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/43788852/is-nexus-repository-3-blob-store-setup-necessary]         This provides, at least, more context to consider, and things to watch out for, that are not reflected in the current documentation, and is a gotcha that has bitten myself, along with many others, who are still waiting on:    https://issues.sonatype.org/browse/NEXUS-12016          """

summary: "Support for ""system accounts"""
description: """Scenario:    For our CI environment we'd like to have dedicated credentials per team to allow for uploading built artifacts to Nexus. This is to ensure that each team only deploy what they should be allowed to (for example their groupId).    All user accounts are in an external Nexus realm (like LDAP). The idea would be to have the CI accounts in a different realm, but possibly there could also be scenarios where those accounts exist in the same realm as the user accounts.    In any case, these CI system accounts should never be used to log into the Nexus UI. Currently (v3.18.1) any user account created which have upload privs are also allowed to log into the UI. It's also then possible to change the name etc of that account; we don't want to allow that. (Or am I configuring something wrong?)    Another thing that we'd like is to be able to do is to get a user token for the ci system account. But one shouldn't have to log in as that user (as we don't want that), but be able to retrieve the token as an admin. The user token would for example be required if """"Require user token for repository authentication"""" is enabled.    Other detail would be that a ci system account doesn't have first and last name, but just a name (one field). Not a big issue, but still."""
comments: "bq. we'd like to have dedicated credentials per team to allow for uploading built artifacts to Nexus. This is to ensure that each team only deploy what they should be allowed to (for example their groupId).    Technically this is already possible to restrict an account with the appropriate privileges.    bq. Another thing that we'd like is to be able to do is to get a user token for the ci system account. But one shouldn't have to log in as that user (as we don't want that), but be able to retrieve the token as an admin.    A security best practice ( and something currently enforced ) is to not make it possible for a token to be retrieved by any user other than the user account tied to the token. I don't think we are going to change that. Sounds like what you really need is NEXUS-12653 officially implemented so the credentials for a service account can retrieve via REST a user token. Make sense?    bq. All user accounts are in an external Nexus realm (like LDAP). The idea would be to have the CI accounts in

summary: "document supported S3 encryption strategies and how to use them"
description: """The page at https://help.sonatype.com/repomanager3/configuration/storage-guide#StorageGuide-S3BlobStore states:    """"The bucket can use server-side encryption with KMS key management transparently.  Other methods of server side encryption are not supported.""""    That is the full extent of how to use bucket encryption with S3 blobstores and what is supported. """"Other methods"""" is vague. """"KMS"""" can mean several different things.    There is no clear statement describing how to enable encryption on an existing bucket and also how to update S3 blobstores config already existing in NXRM.    h4. Expected    Document exactly what to do to use the three encryption mechanisms ( SSE-C, SSE-S3, SSE-KMS ) described here for new blobstores or existing blobstores:    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/serv-side-encryption.html    If something wasn't tested or supported, make clear note of what that is.  """

summary: "scripting should support mapping roles from non-default sources"
description: """org.sonatype.nexus.security.SecurityApi has an addRole method:        This method does not allow specifying the role source, the implementation uses the default source (Default Authorization realm)    While it can work that you can create a role in the default source, and still have that picked up by LDAP or crowd, this seems to be by accident only, is not intuitive and a compatibility byproduct from Nexus 2.    This implicit byproduct is not a good long term approach.    Users want to map roles explicitly to LDAP and CROWD, not the default realm and providing a way to specify the role source would allow that."""

summary: "Add ability to filter view of nexus via a time window"
description: """One of the tasks one wants to do is to be able to reproduce a build based on the state of the repository on a particular timestamp. It is possible to do so with fixed version ranges in the build tools (maven, gradel, docker, etc ) but this is very problematic as not all tools can be configured this way. And of course, mistakes could happen.    A better way would be to allow the repository manager limit the objects visible to build tools based on a timestamp criteria. Thus none of the tools need to change because Nexus (the source of truth) could be trusted to manage the view.    Offhand I could think of a number of ways to make this functionality accessible:    1) decorate the URL passed to nexus. For example:    instead of [https://host:port/...|https://hostport/]    support [https://host:port/timestamp/...|https://hostport/]    A user would modify the URL used in maven docker/npm accordingly.    2) Allow one to create a proxy in nexus where a a configuration element is the timestamp.    A user would create a proxy that has the correct setting.    3) create a content selector that applies to all repo types and allows the specification of the time stamp.    In all cases, one should be able to specify as many timestamp specifications as needed for a large number of states to be recreated as needed.    Of course, if artifacts are deleted, the system would not be able to reproduce the complete state. But that is an acceptable limitation."""
comments: "[~<USER>, it is possible (but not super convenient as it's not well documented) to use the 'q' parameter of our REST search API to pass in an arbitrary elastic search query. This would let you at least iterate through repository content filtered by (say) publication time. This isn't the same thing as a repository endpoint that an npm or maven client would use, however, which is what I assume you're wanting if you're trying to replicate previous builds.    Can you explain what need this is meeting? If you're not concerned about deleted artifacts, it seems like you're mostly trying to exclude more recent versions of dependencies. Are you trying to replicate old SNAPSHOT builds? Do you have builds which have open-ended dependency version ranges, which pick up later versions when you rebuild them? (If so, have you thought about forbidding this?)" [SEP] "We do have builds that use version ranges, and so the actual versions that get picked up is dependent on the contents of Nexus at any given time.    As we h

summary: "Repository Management API missing yum proxy"
description: """  With recent deprecation of scripting the preferred way to manage repositories is through API. Thank you for making API available, they seem a lot saner than scripting way.     But, management of YUM proxies is still missing from repo management API. Seems like I can manage any type of repository/group except for yum proxy    Is this just an oversight, or is there a deeper reason why yum proxy is different from anything else?    Thank you!"""
comments: "It's really neither.  Our REST API isn't complete yet and we have a bunch of stories we're slowly intergrating over time.  We have an internal ticket for yum proxy (and group) but I didn't see a public facing one, so opening this to use as that."

summary: "add task that can Restore Asset/Component metadata to component database from Blob Store contents"
description: """h4. Background  In a number of situations, users will need to repair their component metadata from the disaster recovery information stored in the blob store properties.  - component metadata in database is a complete write-off (unrecoverally corrupt OrientDB, no usable backup)  - component metadata is functional, was restored from an earlier point than the blob stores - so there are blobs with no component metadata    h4. Acceptance    - Admins can repair/rebuild component database metadata from blobs  - Component metadata (sufficient to use the format) is created  - This process can be run on a subset of repositories, and/or blob stores  - target Maven2 format repositories initially, extendable to other formats in the future"""
comments: "A task has been added named ""Create Restore Asset/Component metadata from Blob Store Task"".    You can only select a blob store with this task, not specific repositories.    *The task effectively only works on Maven 2 format repositories for now.*    The task assumes that repositories referenced in blob store properties files will exist already and be in the correct format, if they don't, a WARN message is logged.    "

summary: "provide REST API to expire specific entries from not found cache and age timeouts"
description: """Three main features affect whether Nexus will check a remote site for new content:    Metadata Max Age  Component Max Age  Not Found Cache    Configuring these to perfect values in repository configuration to avoid build failures when new content is available at the remote site is not a science, but an art.    Currently there is a UI button an admin can use to Expire Cache on an entire repository. Where a proxy repository contains large numbers of already cached content, checking the remote for all of these is needlessly expensive, when only one specific package may have failed a build.    _Using the component or asset delete API does not seem like a good solution either_ - it does too much and the interface requires knowing the internal UUID of a specific asset, which is going to require a separate REST call. The goal is not to delete what is already cached anyways ( may cause other build failures unrelated), merely ask Nexus to treat the cache as expired for existing cached items.    There should be a way to expire cache of specific paths and their children assets rather than only all or nothing.    h4. Expected    There should be a repository format generic REST API that could be used to expire specific items in the not found cache and those assets subject to age timeouts configured on a repository.    Nexus 2.x had an Expire Repository Cache scheduled task that one could specify both the repository and the """"path"""" to affect."""

summary: "Help users by choosing a default blobstore for them"
description: """When users create a new repository, they have to choose a blobstore for that repository. Currently, we leave that field blank in all cases, even when there’s only a single option for people to choose from. We can do better.    Proposal:    1) If only one blobstore exists, select it  2) If there is more than one blobstore, select the one named 'default', if it exists.    Rationale:    1) If only one blobstore exists, users have no other option, so it seems like busywork to force them to do so.  2) If there’s a blobstore named ‘default’, we can infer that this is the most common option, and select it by default. If users wish to choose a different blobstore, they can do so. If users don’t want a default, they can refrain from using that blobstore name.    POC:    https://github.com/sonatype/nexus-oss/pull/1584"""
comments: "Blobstore functionality isn’t fully baked, so any solution to this is likely premature."

description: """Manual routing functionality existed in Nexus 2.x, but this functionality does not exit in Nexus 3.x.    [Manual Routing in Nexus 2.x|https://help.sonatype.com/repomanager2/configuration/managing-routing#ManagingRouting-ManualRoutingConfiguration]    Manual routing would allow performance gains when requesting through groups."""

It is not necessary to look for internal organizational Maven artifacts from external proxy repositories." summary: "Display module name of jar"
description: """With Java9/Jigsaw a new entity is introduced: the module name.  In case a jar has a module-info.class, the name can be extracted from this content.  In case there's no module-info.class, there's a strategy to extract the name based on the filename.  http://cr.openjdk.java.net/~mr/jigsaw/spec/api/java/lang/module/ModuleFinder.html#of-java.nio.file.Path...- should help in detecting the name.  Nexus is probably one of those application used by developers trying to discover the modulename for any jar."""
comments: "[~<USER>, what sort of use cases do you see being most useful here? A column in search results? A search criterion? Tucked in asset attributes?" [SEP] "We should think carefully about about loading classes that are part of the content; this sounds like a potential security hole if we're firing off static initializers from proxied content of unknown provenance." [SEP] "With Java 9 and beyond developers will start to add module descriptors to their project, but it is kind of <USER>to discover the name of the module.  For the maven-dependency-plugin I've improved the resolve-goal to show it, but I guess search.maven.org will be one of the main resources.     Using ModuleFinder.of() should not load classes, only parse:  - module-info.class  - META-INF/MANIFEST.MF  - the filename.    In the end, I think it is a different kind of Dependency Information, e.g.    *Java module system*  requires org.foo.bar;" [SEP] "Thanks, that makes sense, Robert.    Do you see this becoming a maven _coordinate_, where end-use

summary: "document best practices to migrate existing LDAP based security to SAML"
description: """Existing customers using LDAP user accounts and role/group mappings in NXRM want to migrate to using SAML instead.    Some questions being asked are:    1) Will the existing users be mapped correctly after the migration? To map it properly what are the things to be taken care of.   2) Any other things (other than users) to take care of during the migration.   3) what changes are required to be done at the Nexus level.    Customers are looking for best practices converting to SAML from other realm configurations.  h4. Expected    Publish a guide with advice on how to migrate from LDAP or other external realms to using SAML instead.    The doc should document the following:   # how to copy LDAP user principals to SAML   # how to map any LDAP permissions to SAML users   # -how to create the same user tokens the LDAP users have for the same SAML user-   # how to test and validate"""
comments: "Related to bullet 3 we've run into issues wrt the user tokens, see NEXUS-24936. One workaround is to invalidate them, but that forces all users to recreate."

summary: "Task to clean old packages on specific repository"
description: """Our CI system (TeamCity) build two type of branches for our npm packages : Develop and Master (simple gitflow process nothing special).  After each correct build CI deploys on two different repository/feed on nexus server: Nightly Feed and Stable Feed.     What we just would like to have is a task to remove old packages on Nightly Feed because packages number is so high. What we found is that a separate feed for nightly is the only way to delete in one shots all nightly and reduce server space requirements.....so right now we just delete feed and recreate it every month."""
comments: "I made this title specific as we have existing tickets for raw and docker.  If it was meant to be generic, feel free to revert or let us know, however I imagine there's a chance the formats will be tackled individually." [SEP] "Actually we have same approach for nuget repository....so i think best is to implements new task where user could select different type of repository (hosted)" [SEP] "[~<USER> I've reverted to generic, however, I suspect this will get broken out (tho may be wrong).  Thanks for the clarify!" [SEP] "Should be included in development with general cleanup effort for NXRM3." If there are specific requests once you see, please raise new tickets (if none exist already)."

summary: "add log messages that help monitor if the proxy repository not found cache is overrun"
description: """Per NEXUS-12677, there are no obvious messages when the proxy repository not found cache has been exceeded or evictions have occurred.    The only real way to tell ( other than actively monitoring the JMX exposed mbeans for ehcache) is a debug message detailing the response for the item you've requested and then you would have to know it has been previously been requested to know that an eviction had occurred (really not useful in a support or diagnostic context).    Trying to force ehcache to log evictions using only the logging config in Nexus does not yield anything useful.    A possible solution would be to extend Cache Event Listener and use that to keep an eye on specific caches? Another idea is a thread which simply inspects the stats which are already being gathered on a regular interval, although has tradeoffs in not being real time.    h4. Expected    - actively monitor cache statistics  for every proxy repository not found cache ( negative-cache )  - at regular intervals determine the effectiveness of each not found cache based on stats ( algorithm TBD )  - in situations where  the caches size is not being effective ( ie. too many misses on a too frequent basis ) log a message indicating this inside nexus.log at WARN level    """

summary: "Add more attributes to REST resource for asset"
description: """The current asset resource as of 3.7 is    This is missing a lot of the available attributes for the asset. I am recommending to add the following:   * content type   * checksums (sha1 for all formats, possibly other checksums as support?)   * an map of attributes for the format (more below)   * possibly (up for debate):   ** etag (tbd as we have it in ES, not sure about Orient)   ** last modified    A sample maven payload:    This would mean the 'maven2' attribute would be dynamic. i.e. would be called 'npm' for npm format, 'nuget' for nuget, etc... and the contents would vary by format. Alternatively we could <USER>code it to 'attributes' or something, but for API design you usually want to have consistency within a sub-resource. So I would prefer a changing name over changing attributes. Essentially a client has to grab the 'format' value and then find an attribute named that."""
comments: "Hello,    For the maven part of asset's attribute, we also need the classifer.    Looking forward to testing the API change.    Thank you." [SEP] "*last_modified* is an important attribute to have         and a nice to have would be {{Blob created}} / {{Blob updated}} for the docker format" [SEP] "Having Blob created/updated in the attibutes via rest api will be very helpful." [SEP] "+1    Would love to make use of this API for querying yum packages too. Specifically, I'm looking to query the description of packages. And getting access to the full attribute set would be the best case.    Happy to <USER>test too if you need test subjects :)" [SEP] "This would also be v useful for PyPI, especially while we await support for the [pypi json api|https://warehouse.pypa.io/api-reference/json.html] (https://issues.sonatype.org/browse/NEXUS-22828)."

summary: "Implement Nuget V3 Group/Hosted ""Registration pages and leaves"""
description: """According to documentation: [https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/api/registration-base-url-resource#registration-pages-and-leaves]    h2. *Group*    Currently, the implementation creates pages that include items instead of links.  The implementation creates pages that include items because pages must be merged, so implementation gets all items and merges them.    *Technical notes.* At the moment group repository implementation works as follows.  When some response contains page without items, then group implementation gets items, next all items will be merged. So, the result page will include or contain pages with items.    Current problem can be resolved as follows:  Implementation should get items, merge them, and after page should include links instead of items if it is necessary and upper and lower versions should be recalculated.  h2. *Hosted*    Currently, hosted creates one page.    Actual: If was uploaded 155 different version for one package. For example 155 different versions of angularjs          *Actual:*    *Expected:* if leaves more than 128               """
comments: "Currently works without any functional restrictions for *NuGet Version: 5.5.0.6382*"

summary: "Extend proxy cooperation across HA nodes"
description: """NEXUS-13378 introduced proxy cooperation between threads in the same node, to avoid the same content being downloaded multiple times.    This story will look at supporting proxy cooperation across HA nodes, potentially using Hazelcast to perform the cooperation. Since hazelcast is only available in HA this will likely involve different implementations for the local and HA case, sharing the same common interface."""

summary: "Use S3 URLs rather than proxying"
description: """Instead of proxying the data from S3 we can probably improve cost and performance by generating an expiring link to download the resources from S3 instead.    I am envisioning that it is just an option to tick off rather than a redo of the blob store.  It should provide a warning stating that the resources MAY be exposed to the outside as the links being generated are public links even if they expire.    It should have a couple of options to specify the expiration period and a minimum expiration period remaining before to request a new token."""
comments: "This is an interesting idea, and we have actually looked into it but not yet prioritized it for work. I'm opening this to attract votes."

summary: "partial update oriented REST APIs"
description: """NXRM 3 has an automation focused and supported REST API. NXRM 2 does not.    A complaint from NXRM 2 users is the REST API there did not allow """"partial updates"""". """"Partial updates"""" means the REST API caller provide enough info to identify the resource to update, and then supply only the config of that resource they want to change, rather than the entire pre-existing payload. NXRM 2 API was designed for the UI ONLY which in theory had all the original information to submit back to update.     In NXRM 3, we would like to do better.    Examples of """"partial updates"""" using a REST API would be:    - add a new group repo member, without needing to supply the entire list of members  - change a hosted repo deployment policy to READ ONLY  - change a proxy repo Blocked status  - remove a privilege from a Role  - add a role to another role    Variations which are more subtle would be:    - delete a role from a user, and not erring if that role does not exist - since the goal is make sure the role is not assigned to that user  - add a privilege to a role and not erring if that priv is already added to the role, since the intent of the action is already met    Lack of the """"partial updates"""" feature increases the likelihood of unforced errors because the caller needs to pull all data first, modify one small part of it and submit back to the server and hope that no other user modified the record in between    h4. Expected    Implement partial update capability to all REST API endpoints which would have the most positive impact to users.  """
comments: "Agreed. Many of the POST endpoints should be implemented as a PATCH endpoint.     For example, a PATCH would allow the modification of a specific item instead of requiring the entire object.  Currently, to update an APT repository you have to include the signing keypair and passphrase, which shouldn't be required when you just need to update the distribution, or the writePolicy. This becomes a security issue having to include the keypair and passphrase and also runs the risk of corruption or creating errors in the repository configuration.    PATCH .../<USER>repositories/apt/hosted/\{repositoryName}"

summary: "Add repository import/export capabilities"
description: """Hi,    Even though Internet access is taken for granted, I sadly work with a lot of customers that do not give any Internet access to the developers. The software must run without Internet access and they believe (wrongly in my opinion) that this can be ensured only if the developers themselves did not have Internet access while building the software.    So Nexus 2 has been a life saver for us. Whenever we need new libraries, we request them from Nexus in proxy mode in a network with Internet access. Then we export the artifacts from the file system and manually import them in the """"offline"""" Nexus in a hosted repository and rebuild the metadata and index. We do that for maven and npm repositories.    Sadly the move to a blob store in Nexus 3 makes it impossible to replicate our work procedure if we migrate to Nexus 3.    I'd like if you could add a repository export/import feature in Nexus 3 like in Artifactory, where an import would merge the new content into an existing repository. Also a filter on the export by date would be great.    Regards  """
comments: "There is a Maven 2 format repository import/export strategy discussed in this article: https://support.sonatype.com/hc/en-us/articles/236210187" [SEP] "We are looking to migrate our maven repositories from Artifcatory to Nexus 3. However, currently there does not seem to be a away to do this easily." [SEP] "[~<USER> Hi Shaun!  We have an internal task to have this easier for NXRM3, I will see about getting it made public.  However, you're right, currently there's no easy way.  We recommend people who are looking to move from Artifactory to NXRM3 follow our documentation to move to NXRM2 (here: http://books.sonatype.com/nexus-book/reference/artifactory.html) and then upgrade to NXRM3 (documented here: http://books.sonatype.com/nexus-book/reference3/upgrading.html#upgrade-version-two-three). summary: "deleting quarantined assets may not successfully notify IQ server using HTTP DELETE when blocked by security software"
description: """Construct a policy in IQ server with FAIL action on Repository stage.    Add an HTTP proxy server in Nexus Repository Manager that returns 200 status code, Content-Type: text/html, and a custom Server header value in response to DELETE requests to /rest/integration/repositories/*, but actually prevents that request from ever reaching IQ server. This can be simulated using Charles proxy and breakpoints.    Connect IQ server and Nexus Repository Manager together and enable quarantine ( Firewall )   on a proxy repository.     Make a proxy repository request which successfully quarantines an asset requested through the Nexus proxy repository.    Now delete the asset in Nexus repository manager. Internally this means IQ server will need to know about the deletion. It 'hears' about it because Nexus Repository Manager will send a HTTP DELETE request to IQ server.    If the HTTP Proxy Server that repository manager uses interferes with DELETE requests outbound, then the response may be returned with status code 200, even though IQ server never received the request.    We have seen this scenario with Symantec Web Server security filtering for one.    h4. Detection    Set the log level to {{DEBUG}} for the {{org.apache.http}} logger inside Nexus repository Manager.  Delete the asset.  Examine the nexus.log, specifically for the outbound delete request to IQ server and the response headers coming back - example:        Unexpected are:    - status code is 200 which is the same code IQ server would return, so repository manager thinks it successfully told IQ server about this deletion, but the 200 is actually coming from the HTTP Proxy Server  - Server response header is not from IQ server, but SWS ( Symantec Web Security)  - content type is HTML, IQ server does not respond with HTML content type to that call  - examining the IQ server request.log shows no inbound DELETE request being received    h4. Workaround    - Add the IQ server to the HTTP Proxy server non-proxy hosts to bypass the aggressive Network firewall manipulating HTTP DELETE requests outbound.  - modify the HTTP Proxy servers aggressive policy to ALLOW outbound HTTP DELETE requests    h4. Expected    There are situations where applications are forced to NOT bypass the configured HTTP proxy server when accessing other servers due to company policy and the Repository manager administrator is not in control of HTTP traffic policies it may enforce.     Ideas to help mitigate this failure when designing application interactions are:    - respond with and expect a 204 for successful HTTP DELETE requests instead of 200 to avoid being tricked 200 indicates success  - examine the content type of the response in addition to status code  - return JSON body indicating success instead of empty 200 response  - return response headers specific to IQ server ( {{X-}} ) that help verify the response came from IQ server and not some other server - understanding the {{Server}} header is NOT a good candidate to check value of as it commonly gets rewritten by reverse proxies    Also:    When a failure is detected, print the response headers at default log levels to more quickly prove the request never reached the intended server - this avoids the burden of enabling additional logging to detect what server interfered with the outbound request.  """

summary: "Improvement:  Component upload REST API option wait for yum metadata rebuild"
description: """Currently generation of yum metadata is delayed for 60 seconds after an rpm has been deployed. The reason for this is that the yum metadata format consists of a handful of potentially very large XML files. These can be very expensive to rebuild, and so we need to avoid overwhelming Nexus Repo with many tasks to rebuild metadata in the event of rapid rpm deployments.    This metadata rebuild behavior causes a problem for CI builds that depend on deployed rpm's to be available immediately after the job that builds and deploys them finishes.  Subsequent jobs very often fail because the newly deployed rpm file is not immediately available through the hosted repository.  Currently the only workaround is to introduce a delay period into the CI job after deployment, but this can never work with complete effectiveness, because it isn't always possible to predict when the rpm metadata rebuild task will start, or how long it will take to finish.    To handle this use case, I propose a to add a way to make a CI client block until any pending yum metadata updates have completed.     One way to do that would be to add an option to yum deployment in component REST API. This flag would tell the POST request to not return until the yum metadata rebuild has been completed.  In this way, CI jobs could be built which reliably deploy and consume rpm files.    Another possibility would be a new REST API that will return immediately if there are no pending yum metadata updates for the target repository, or if there are pending updates, will block until they complete.               """

summary: "nexus should provide a list of artifacts with versions in one go"

summary: "Disable OrientDB live query support"
description: """We don't use the https://orientdb.com/docs/2.2.x/Live-Query.html feature and disabling it completely (ie. removing it totally as a DB hook) will give a small performance boost.    It will also guarantee to remove future log-spam issues caused by {{OLiveQueryHook}}'s erroneous assumption that there will be a valid DB thread-local when it's called - which isn't always the case depending on the scenario.    This has led in the past to a number of log-spam reports, mostly on HA but also on a couple of non-HA systems under stress. The previous reports should have been fixed now by upstream changes, but disabling the live-query feature would protect us from similar issues in the future."""

summary: "Process search index updates in the background in a non-blocking fashion"
description: """It was noticed that during elastic search index updates, a considerably large number of threads were getting blocked as a result of contention for the org.elasticsearch.action.bulk.BulkProcessor's Mutual Exclusion lock. This causes progressively slower response times from the application.    We solve this problem by decoupling the request for search index updates from the processing of those requests. This ensures that requesting threads can return to their callers quickly thereby keeping the system responsive.        Example thread stacks which show the problem:              """
comments: "Fixed."

summary: "Add Support for Multi-Architecture Docker Images (Manifest List)"
description: """To allow support of images across multiple operating systems and architectures, Docker has introduced multi-architecture images (aka manifest list or fat manifest). As stated in [https://developer.ibm.com/linuxonpower/2017/07/27/create-multi-architecture-docker-image/:]    _An individual manifest describes the contents of one image, and a manifest list lets you group multiple images together. After the creation of a manifest list, anyone (from the project owners to their user-base) can use the manifest list name where they once used an individual image name. When a user does a docker pull or docker run, the docker engine does the work of selecting which image to pull based on the operating system and architecture on which it is running. As a result, a project’s users no longer have to worry about finding the name of the image that will work for their platform_    An example of a manifest list can be found in the Docker spec: [https://docs.docker.com/registry/spec/manifest-v2-2/#example-manifest-list]    The expectation is that Nexus should support both the hosting and proxying of multi-arch images. This will also require adding support for the manifest list format type *application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json*    It should be possible to both push such an image and more importantly pull an image via Nexus along the lines of the above description i.e. """"docker engine does the work of selecting which image to pull based on the operating system and architecture on which it is running"""".      See below for code changes done for tools like podman and cri-o to fix the issue in those products.  https://github.com/containers/image/commit/ca5fe04cb38a1f0e0b960e9388a3c6372efd215a  """
comments: "Are there plans from Sonatype to implement support for multi-architecture docker registeries in Nexus 3?" [SEP] "From NXRM 3.22.0 Docker repositories support the following:    * *Proxying* manifest lists.  * Uploading manifest lists to a *hosted* repository.  * Pulling manifest lists via a *group* repository.    Note: Pulling via a group repository *will not* merge manifest lists from the members members." [SEP] "I have updated to 3.23.0. Is it not available in the nexus3 self-hosted image distributed via dockerhub?  " [SEP] "The docker image and the download binary should be the same. summary: "include server thread id in request.log"
description: """Thread dumps are a common problem diagnosis tool. The request.log gives a picture of the activity the server is receiving retroactively.    Jetty Server delegates request processing to threads in its thread pool. These threads start with """"qtp"""".    While a thread dump will show you the thread id and its stack, what is virtually impossible to map is what request in the request.log ( assuming the thread finishes ) was associated wth the thread in a thread dump.    If the request qtp threadid name was included in each request.log line as a field, one could  in theory retroactively compare thread dumps + the timestamp it was taken to a request.log and isolate the specific requests contributing to some problem.    h4. Expected    Include the thread id name of the request processing thread in the request.log by default.    """
comments: "The thread ID is now added at the end of the logline. Old format:  {{127.0.0.1 - - [03/Apr/2019:12:06:34 +0100] ""POST /service/extdirect/poll/rapture_State_get HTTP/1.1"" 200 1224 77 9 ""Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_14_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/73.0.3683.86 Safari/537.36""}}    New format:  {{127.0.0.1 - - [03/Apr/2019:12:41:09 +0100] ""GET /service/outreach/nexus.js HTTP/1.1"" 200 - 6712 19 ""Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_14_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/73.0.3683.86 Safari/537.36"" [qtp1121507949-207]}}"

Tested with a user that has the following privileges:  * nx-search-read  * nx-repository-view-maven2--read  * nx-repository-view-maven2--browse     Need a way to disable access to """"Analyze Application"""" functionality by privilege or capability  """
